8.1.title: Types of Joints
objectives: Explain joint classification by structural binding tissue and functional movement degree.
Describe structural features and examples of fibrous joints.
Describe structural features and examples of cartilaginous joints.
Describe the general anatomy of a synovial joint.
Distinguish the six types of synovial joints and name an example of each.

classification_intro.definition: Joints (articulations) are functional junctions between bones.
functions: Bind parts of the skeletal system together
Permit parts of the skeleton to change shape during childbirth
Enable body movement in response to skeletal muscle contractions

structural_types.Fibrous: Composed of dense fibrous connective tissue; no joint cavity present.
structural_types.Cartilaginous: Composed of hyaline cartilage or fibrocartilage; no joint cavity present.
structural_types.Synovial: Contains a fluid-filled joint cavity; permits free movement.

fibrous_joints.name: Syndesmosis
fibrous_joints.structure: Bones bound by a long cord or sheet of fibrous connective tissue (ligament).
fibrous_joints.function_detail: Provides give or slight movement but no true active movement.

examples.bones: Acromial end of clavicle and acromion process of scapula

fibrous_joints.name: Suture
fibrous_joints.structure: Bones bound by thin, short fibrous connective tissue fibers.

fibrous_joints.name: Gomphosis
fibrous_joints.structure: Cone-shaped bony process held inside a bony socket by short periodontal ligaments.

examples.bones: Tooth root within the alveolar fossa (socket) of mandible or maxilla

cartilaginous_joints.name: Synchondrosis
cartilaginous_joints.structure: Bones bound by a temporary or permanent plate of hyaline cartilage.
cartilaginous_joints.function_detail: Immovable; serves as sites of bone growth in youth, which eventually ossify into synostoses.

examples.bones: First rib and the manubrium of the sternum
Diaphysis and epiphysis of growing long bones

cartilaginous_joints.name: Symphysis
cartilaginous_joints.structure: Bones covered by hyaline cartilage and joined by a resilient pad or plate of fibrocartilage.
cartilaginous_joints.function_detail: Slightly movable; acts as a compressible shock absorber.

examples.bones: Pubic portions of the coxal bones
Adjacent vertebrae of the spinal column

general_features.description: A thin layer of hyaline cartilage covering the articulating surface of each bone to reduce wear and friction.
A double-layered sheath enclosing the joint cavity. Consists of an outer tough fibrous capsule (continuous with periosteum) and an inner synovial membrane.
Loose connective tissue lining the inner fibrous capsule. Covers all internal joint surfaces except articular cartilage. Secretes synovial fluid.
The potential space between articulating bones filled with lubricating fluid.
A viscous, clear lubricating fluid that reduces friction, provides lubrication, nourishes the articular cartilage, and contains phagocytes.
Strong cords of fibrous tissue that bind bone to bone, strengthening the joint capsule (can be intrinsic/capsular thickenings).

accessory_structures.name: Fatty pads
accessory_structures.description: Adipose tissue collections that act as cushions, found in hip and knee joints.

accessory_structures.name: Menisci
accessory_structures.description: Articular discs of fibrocartilage that partially or completely divide the joint cavity into compartments to distribute weight and absorb shock.

accessory_structures.name: Bursa
accessory_structures.description: Flattened fibrous sacs lined with synovial membrane and containing fluid. Act as ball bearings to cushion muscles, tendons, ligaments, and skin over bony projections.
locations: Between muscle, tendon, ligament and bone

synovial_joint_types.description: The globular head of one bone fits into the cup-shaped socket of another.
synovial_joint_types.movement_capability: Multiaxial; most freely movable synovial type. Allows all angular movements and rotation.

examples.articulation: Head of humerus and glenoid cavity of scapula
Head of femur and acetabulum of coxal bone

synovial_joint_types.description: Ovoid condyle of one bone fits into the elliptical cavity of another.
synovial_joint_types.movement_capability: Biaxial; permits all angular motions except rotation.

examples.articulation: Medial and lateral condyles of femur and tibia

synovial_joint_types.description: Articulating surfaces are nearly flat or slightly curved, permitting sliding or back-and-forth motion.

examples.articulation: Patellar surface of femur and patella
Capitulum of humerus and head of radius

synovial_joint_types.description: Convex surface of one bone fits into the concave surface of another, restricting movement to a single plane.
synovial_joint_types.movement_capability: Uniaxial; permits bending and straightening actions.

examples.articulation: Trochlea of humerus and trochlear notch of ulna
Femur and tibia condyles acting together as a hinge

synovial_joint_types.description: Cylindrical surface of one bone rotates within a ring formed of bone and ligament.
synovial_joint_types.movement_capability: Uniaxial; limits movement to rotation around a central axis.

examples.articulation: First and second cervical vertebrae (Atlas and Axis)

synovial_joint_types.description: Articulating surfaces have both concave and convex regions, resembling a saddle on a rider.
synovial_joint_types.movement_capability: Biaxial; allows various angular movements and specialized opposition.

8.2.title: Types of Joint Movements
objectives: Explain how skeletal muscles contract across joints, pulling insertion toward origin.
Identify and define types of joint movements.

mechanics.origin: Part of muscle attached to the immovable or anchored bone.
mechanics.insertion: Part of muscle attached to the movable bone across a joint.
mechanics.contraction_principle: When a muscle contracts, its insertion is pulled toward its origin, producing movement at the joint.

movement_dictionary.definition: Decreasing the angle between two articulating bones (e.g., bending the head toward the chest, bending the elbow).
Increasing the angle between two articulating bones (e.g., straightening the knee, straightening the elbow).
Increasing the angle greater than the normal anatomical position (e.g., bending the head backward).
Movement at the ankle that brings the toes upward, closer to the shin.
Movement at the ankle that points the toes downward, toward the sole of the foot.
Moving a limb or body part away from the midline of the body (e.g., raising arm laterally).
Moving a limb or body part toward the midline of the body (e.g., lowering arm back to trunk).
Turning a bone along its own longitudinal axis (e.g., shaking the head 'no').
Moving a limb in a circular, cone-shaped path where the distal end moves in a circle while the proximal end remains stable.
Rotation of the forearm so the palm faces upward or anteriorly (e.g., thumbs up position).
Rotation of the forearm so the palm faces downward or posteriorly (e.g., thumbs down position).
Turning the sole of the foot outward, laterally.
Turning the sole of the foot inward, medially.
Moving a body part forward in a horizontal plane (e.g., thrusting the chin forward).
Moving a body part backward in a horizontal plane (e.g., pulling the head back into anatomical alignment).
Raising a body part vertically (e.g., shrugging the shoulders).
Lowering a body part vertically (e.g., opening the mouth, depressing the mandible).

8.3.title: Examples of Synovial Joints
objectives: Describe the structures and supporting ligaments of the shoulder joint.
Describe the structural features of the elbow, hip, and knee joints.

components.articulation: Glenoid cavity of scapula and head of humerus.
components.details: Most freely movable joint. Surrounded by the rotator cuff (tendons/muscles) and multiple bursae. Allowed movements in any angular plane.

components.articulation: Acromial end of clavicle and acromion process of scapula.
Trochlea of humerus and trochlear notch of ulna.
components.details: Extremely stable, reinforced by many ligaments. Restricts movement to flexion and extension.

components.articulation: Capitulum of humerus and head of radius.
Head of femur and acetabulum of coxal bone.
components.details: Features the fovea capitis on the femur head. Highly stable due to deep socket and large reinforcing ligaments. Allows same movements as shoulder but with a reduced range.

components.articulation: Medial condyles of femur and tibia (one joint), lateral condyles of femur and tibia (second joint).
components.details: Functionally works as a hinge. Permits flexion, extension, and slight rotation.

components.articulation: Patellar surface of femur and patella.

reinforcing_structures.details: Menisci reshape tibial condyles for a better fit and act as shock absorbers. Multiple bursae lubricate the joint.

8.4.title: Life-Span Changes
objectives: Describe age-related changes occurring in joints across the life span.

changes.change: Fontanels (fibrous joints) of the skull close and harden.
Epiphyseal plates (synchondroses) ossify and harden, stopping bone growth.
Collagen structures change, causing progressive stiffening of the joints.
Fibrocartilage pads lose water content, decreasing the flexibility of the intervertebral joints and knees.

changes.impact: Regular physical activity decreases the onset and severity of joint stiffening.
